coredata.dc:title: Nanoengineering of ultrathin N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 S-scheme heterojunction for enhanced photodegradation of antibiotics as emerging contaminants: Mechanism insight and toxicity assessment

coredata.dc:description: Constructing low-cost and wide visible light response S-scheme heterojunctions is crucial for their photocatalytic efficiency and practical applications. Herein, a novel N-CQDs/UBWO composite was designed by combining nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) obtained from bio-waste lignin with Bi2WO6 ultrathin nanosheets (UBWO) using in-situ hydrothermal approach. The work function analyses, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and in-situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) evidenced an S-scheme charge transfer mechanism between N-CQD and UBWO duringphotocatalyticreactions, which endows the composite system a high photocatalytic redox and charge space separation capabilities. Besides, the up-conversion properties of N-CQDs render N-CQDs/UBWO composites anenhanced visible light response. Therefore, the optimized 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO S-scheme heterojunction exhibited favorable tetracycline degradation performance, with a degradation efficiency of 85.0 % within 40 min of reaction time, and first-order rate constant (k) of 2.6 and 20.3 times greater than that of UBWO and N-CQDs, respectively. Furthermore, referring to Fukui function calculations and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), degraded products and three degradation routes for tetracycline were proposed. The results of the toxicity estimation software tool (T.E.S.T) and mung bean cultivation demonstrated that the intermediate products of tetracycline degradation are of low toxicity. This study provides insights into designing superior S-scheme heterojunctions using CQDs derived from waste biomass for green and efficient removal of antibiotics from wastewater.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Constructing low-cost and wide visible light response S-scheme heterojunctions is crucial for their photocatalytic efficiency and practical applications. Herein, a novel N-CQDs/UBWO composite was designed by combining nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) obtained from bio-waste lignin with Bi2WO6 ultrathin nanosheets (UBWO) using in-situ hydrothermal approach. The work function analyses, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and in-situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) evidenced an S-scheme charge transfer mechanism between N-CQD and UBWO duringphotocatalyticreactions, which endows the composite system a high photocatalytic redox and charge space separation capabilities. Besides, the up-conversion properties of N-CQDs render N-CQDs/UBWO composites anenhanced visible light response. Therefore, the optimized 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO S-scheme heterojunction exhibited favorable tetracycline degradation performance, with a degradation efficiency of 85.0 % within 40 min of reaction time, and first-order rate constant (k) of 2.6 and 20.3 times greater than that of UBWO and N-CQDs, respectively. Furthermore, referring to Fukui function calculations and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), degraded products and three degradation routes for tetracycline were proposed. The results of the toxicity estimation software tool (T.E.S.T) and mung bean cultivation demonstrated that the intermediate products of tetracycline degradation are of low toxicity. This study provides insights into designing superior S-scheme heterojunctions using CQDs derived from waste biomass for green and efficient removal of antibiotics from wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the widespread usage of antibiotics has posed serious impacts on the ecological environment and human health [1]. As common contaminants of emerging concern (CECs), antibiotics have complex molecular structures, biological toxicity, environmental persistence, and bioaccumulation, making them difficult to be effectively eliminated through traditional biochemical treatment processes [2]. To resolve this issue, the visible light-driven photocatalytic reaction is considered a promising antibiotics pollution control technology due to its advantages of being clean and green, mild reaction conditions, and strong free radical oxidation ability [3,4]. According to the basic principles of photocatalytic reactions, their efficiencies were usually determined by three factors including light absorption, separation and migration of photogenerated carriers (e-/h+), and redox reactions on the surface of photocatalysts [5]. Therefore, efficient photocatalytic systems are always constructed by considering these three processes. Among various semiconductor photocatalysts, Bi2WO6 as a representative bismuth-based photocatalytic materials, has aunique layered structure, ultra-low toxicity, stable chemical structure, and visible light response, showing great potential in the field of photocatalytic environmental pollution remediation [6]. However, intrinsic Bi2WO6 still has problems such as anarrow photo-response range (<450 nm), high electron (e-) and hole (h+) recombination rate, and small specific surface area, resulting in low photocatalytic activity, especially for antibiotics removal [7].

To address these limitations of Bi2WO6-based feedstocks, various methods have been reported to enhance their photocatalytic performance (i.e., morphology control [8], doping engineering [9], defect engineering [10], co-catalyst modification [11], heterojunction construction [12], external energy field [13], etc.). Among the aforementioned strategies, heterojunctions engineering has been regarded to be conductive in forming effective photocatalytic feedstocks with a good light response, excellent charge separation, and high redox ability [14,15], especially advanced S-scheme heterojunctions which have shown excellent benefits in antibiotics degradation. In the S-scheme heterojunction, when two semiconductor materials with distinct Fermi levels (Ef) and band structures are in close contact, electrons with higher Ef will flow towards lower Ef until their Ef reach equilibrium, resulting in an internal electric field (IEF) and band bending at the interface of heterojunction [16]. Under illumination, IEF and band bending drive e- and h+ undergo recombination, while strong reducing e- and strong oxidizing h+ are retained [17]. Therefore, efficient separation of e- and h+ in space is achieved, while maintaining high redox capability. It can be noted that constructing S-scheme heterojunctions is a beneficial strategy to improve the photocatalytic efficiency of intrinsic Bi2WO6. Recently, some Bi2WO6-based S-scheme heterojunctions have been found to exhibit enhanced photocatalytic performance (i.e., Bi2WO6/AgInS2, Cs2AgBiBr6/Bi2WO6, BP/Bi2WO6, Cd0.5Zn0.5S/Bi2WO6, BiOBr/Bi2WO6, etc.) [18–22]. Considering the practical application prospects, these Bi2WO6-based S-scheme heterojunctions still suffer from the problem of narrow visible light response range and the need fortoxic or expensive precursor usages for their fabrication.

In recent years, CQDs as novel zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, haveshown significant advantages in the design of efficient photocatalysts due to their quantum size effect, good dispersion, stable chemical structure, low cost, low toxicity, and strong light absorption ability [23,24]. More interestingly, the up-conversion property of CQDs, which converts long-wavelength light into short-wavelength high-energy light, provides anopportunity for expanding the photo-response range of Bi2WO6-based photocatalytic materials [25]. Regarding CQDs production, China generates approximately 900 million tons of waste biomass annually, if these biomasses can be converted into CQDs and combined with Bi2WO6 can lead to the construction of green, low-cost, and efficient photocatalysts. Consequently, CQDs production can minimize the use of toxic or expensive chemicals and promote the green and low-cost preparation of Bi2WO6-based photocatalysts. Recently, several S-scheme heterojunctions based on CQDs have been reported (e.g., CQDs/Cu2O, N-CQDs/Ag3PO4, N-CQDs/S-C3N4, MoS2/ZnIn2S4/CQDs, etc.) indicating the feasibility of S-scheme heterojunctions construction employing rational design of CQDs [26–29]. However, there is still a lack of in-depth research on using waste biomass-derived CQDs to construct superior S-scheme heterojunctions for treating antibiotics in wastewater. To promote further developments in this field, more efforts are needed to construct well-structured CQDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions.

In this work, we successfully constructed a 0D/2D S-scheme heterojunction encompassing Bi2WO6 ultrathin nanosheets decorated with N-CQDs via in-situ hydrothermal method, where the N-CQDs were obtained from bio-waste lignin (Scheme 1 ). The up-conversion properties of N-CQDs endow the S-scheme heterojunction abroad spectral response ability as evidenced by in-situ EPR testing under 700 nm light illumination. The work function analyses including in-situ XPS and EPR provide strong evidence about the S-scheme charge transfer pathway within the N-CQDs/UBWO during the photocatalytic reaction. The synergistic effect of ultrathin structure and S-scheme heterojunction promotes efficient separation of photogenerated charges, endowing them a high photocatalytic redox capability. Benefiting from the above results, this S-scheme heterojunction displays agood photocatalytic degradation rate (85.0 %) towards tetracycline (TC) as amodel of antibiotics under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm), which is greater than those of UBWO and N-CQDs. Moreover, the influence of external factors (e.g., N-CQDs dosage, pH, anions effect) on the photocatalytic removal of TC was evaluated. The mechanism and degradation pathway of photocatalytic degradation of TC, as well as the low toxicity of intermediate products were also elucidated.

Preparation of N-CQDs: 0.2 g of alkali lignin was added and 70 mg of urea in 40 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture was continuously stirred for 2 h until forming a homogeneous mixture. After that, the resulting mixture was transferred to a 100 mL glass beaker and heated in a household microwave oven (800 W) for 15 min. After completing the reaction, the mixture was naturally cooled to ambient temperature. Then, the water solvent was completely evaporated and a brownish-yellow solid was formed in the beaker. To wash the obtained solid, 40 mL of ultrapure water was added to the beaker and the solid was sonicated for 30 min. Finally, after the ultrasonic treatment, the liquid was filtered three times through a 50 nm filter membrane to obtain a transparent yellow–brown liquid which was, then, dried in an 80 ℃ oven for 12 h to form N-CQDs powder.

Synthesis of UBWO: 1 mmol of Na2WO4·2H2O, 2 mmol of Bi (NO3)3·5H2O, and 0.05 g of CTAB were mixed in 75 mL of ultrapure water. Then, after magnetic stirring for 2 h, the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL PTFE-lined hydrothermal reactor and heated at 120 ℃ for 24 h. After completing the reaction, the obtained powder was washed for 5 times with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol and finally dried at 80 ℃ for 10 h to obtain UBWO nanosheets. The preparation of bulk BiW2O6 (PBWO) is similar to the above steps, except forthe addition of CTAB surfactant.

Fabrication of N-CQDs/UBWO: 1 mmol Na2WO4·2H2O, 2 mmol Bi (NO3) 3·5H2O, and 0.05 g CTAB were solubilized in 75 mL of ultrapure water. Then, after continuous stirring for 1 h, different weights of 14, 21 and 28 mg of the synthesized N-CQDs were added to above-solution, respectively. After continuous stirring for another 1 h, adifferent solution was transferred to a 100 mL high-pressure vessel lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 120 ℃ for 24 h. After being naturally cooled, the obtained powder was washed with anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water for 5 times to eliminate residual reactants and then dried under anair atmosphere at 80 ℃ for 10 h. The above preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 , where the sample obtained by adding 14 mg of N-CQDs is labeled as 2 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO, the sample obtained by adding 21 mg of N-CQDs is named as 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO, and the sample obtained by adding 28 mg of N-CQDs is labeled as 4 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO. In addition, adding the same amount of N-CQDs without surfactants as a control in pure water proves that the obtained N-CQDs are stable during hydrothermal processes.

The 50 mL of each antibiotic solution (20 mg/L) was mixed with the 15 mg catalysts under continuous stirring achieving adsorption equilibrium under dark conditions for 40 min. For antibiotics degradation under light irradiation (300 W Xe lamp equipped with a cut 420 nm filter) was used in this study to provide visible light and a bandpass 700 nm filter to furnish near-infrared light. After completing the adsorption of antibiotics in dark conditions, thelight source was turned on for their photodegradation. Then, 3 mL of the reaction solution at regular intervals was filtered using a 0.22 μm syringe to obtain the supernatant for residual antibiotics measurements. The residual concentration of TC was measured using a UV-2600 UV visible spectrophotometer, with a maximum absorption wavelength of 357 nm. The residual concentration of ciprofloxacin (CIP) was measured using LC-2030 high-performance liquid chromatography, in which 1 mL of transparent degradation solution was transferred into a brown chromatographic flask, with a mobile phase consisting of 30 % pure methanol and 70 % formic acid (1 %) aqueous solution at a flow rate of 1.0 mL∙min−1. After that, 10 μL of thesample was injected into an automatic sampler, maintaining the column temperature at 40 °C, and CIP absorption wavelength at 272 nm. After degradation, the residual sample in the container was recovered by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, followed by centrifugal cleaning with purified water and anhydrous ethanol, freeze-drying to obtain the recovered powder, and collecting the dried powder for continued uses.

Detailed information about chemical reagents, characterization equipment and methods, details of photodegradation evaluation, theory calculations and toxicity assessment are given in the Supporting Information.

The structure of N-CQDs obtained from lignin by microwave method was characterized using TEM. As presented in Fig. 1(a), it can be shown that N-CQDs have a spherical shape and are completely dispersed particles. Besides, the HR-TEM micrograph of N-CQDs is displayed in Fig. 1(b), which reveals lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.216 nm, which can attributed to the lattice spacing of the graphite carbon (100) plane [30]. The size distribution histogram of N-CQDs is presented in Fig. 1(c), which shows theaverage particle size of N-CQDs is 7.8 nm. The above results evidence that highly crystalline N-CQDs were synthesized from lignin through a rapid microwave synthesis method.

The differences in microstructure of PBWO, UBWO, and 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO were investigated through the SEM technique. As depicted in Fig. 1(d), the SEM image of PBWO exhibits a flower-shaped morphology composed of nanosheets with a thickness of approximately 30 nm. After adding CTAB surfactant to the hydrothermal synthesis system of PBWO, ultrathin UBWO nanosheet structures were obtained. As presented in Fig. 1(e), the morphology of UBWO is an irregular structure composed of ultrathin nanosheets randomly arranged, resembling blooming petals. It is worth noting that at the same magnification, the thickness of UBWO nanosheets is significantly smaller than that of PBWO. Further introduction of N-CQDs into the CTAB-assisted hydrothermal synthesis system resulted in a 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO composite formation as revealed in Fig. 1(f), with a microstructure similar to UBWO. However, due to the small size of N-CQDs, they were not observed in SEM images. The thickness and structure of UBWO were accurately measured using theAFM technique. As shown in Fig. S1, UBWO presents a two-dimensional nanosheet structure with the thickness of UBWO is approximately 1.5 nm, indicating the synthesis of ultrathin UBWO employing CTAB-assisted hydrothermal conditions.

To further demonstrate the successful construction of N-CQDs/UBWO composites, TEM was employed to visualize the microstructure of the optimized 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO. As displayed in Fig. S2, the TEM image of UBWO shows that its microstructure is an irregular structure composed of nanosheets, with a smooth surface without the presence of small particles. Compared with pure UBWO, the TEM image of 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO consists of nanosheets with a large number of N-CQDs small particles on its surface (as indicated by the red circles in Fig. 1g-h). After enlarging the HR-TEM image as shown in Fig. 1(i), which clearly displays three different lattice stripes on the 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO surface. The region with a stripe spacing of 0.216 nm corresponds to the lattice spacing of N-CQDs (100) planes, while the regions with stripe spacing of 0.318 and 0.275 nm correspond to the lattice spacing of Bi2WO6 (131) and (200) planes, respectively [31]. The element mapping distribution (Fig. S3) exhibits that Bi, W, O, C and N atoms are present in 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO, further confirming the presence of N-CQDs and Bi2WO6. Furthermore, Fig. S4 displays the SAED pattern of 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO. The circles demonstrate the polycrystalline structure of 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO, corresponding to the reflections from the Bi2WO6 (131), (200)/(002), (202), (133), and N-CQDs (100) planes. This fully demonstrates that N-CQDs are tightly loaded on Bi2WO6 nanosheets, which can boost the photogenerated carrier separation over 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO in photocatalytic reactions. This may be due to the easy binding of surface-active groups of N-CQDs to Bi2WO6 nanosheets during the in-situ hydrothermal reaction.

The crystal structures of the synthesized PBWO, UBWO, and 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO were characterized by theXRD technique. As noticed in Fig. 2 (a), the diffraction peaks situated at 28.3°, 32.8°, 47.1°, 56.0°, 58.5°, and 68.8° belong to the crystal planes of Bi2WO6 (131), (200)/(002), (202)/(026), (133), (262), and (400) (JCPDS: 79–2381) [32]. It is worth noting that all samples exhibit diffraction peaks similar to PBWO, indicating that the addition of N-CQDs does not significantly affect the crystalline structure of Bi2WO6. However, no diffraction peaks of N-CQDs are observed in the 3 wt% N-CQDs/UBWO, possibly due to the content of N-CQDs being below the detection limit of 5 % in theXRD instrument [33].

Furthermore, the specific area and pore structure of PBWO, UBWO, and 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO were determined. As presented in Fig. 2(b), the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm exhibits a typical IV type, indicating the formation of microporous and mesoporous structures in the samples [34]. The specific surface areas of PBWO, UBWO, and 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO are 24.41, 35.32, and 49.33 m2/g, respectively, confirming that ultrathin UBWO has a larger surface area than PBWO and can expose more photocatalytic reaction active sites. After introducing N-CQDs on UBWO’s surface, the specific surface area of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO was further increased. The pore size distribution diagram in Fig. 2(c) shows that the pore sizes of the three catalysts are all less than 50 nm, mainly consisting of microporous and mesoporous structures, with pore volumes of 0.129, 0.163, and 0.166 cm3/g, respectively. The 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO exhibits the maximum specific surface area and pore volume, providing sufficient sites for the adsorption of antibiotics and their photocatalytic degradation reactions.

To further determine the surface element composition and valence state of the prepared photocatalyst, and to gain a deeper insight into the interaction between N-CQDs and UBWO, XPS analysis was conducted for UBWO and 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO. XPS full spectrum (Fig. 2d) reveals the existence of Bi, W, C, O, and N atoms in 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO, further indicating the successful preparation of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. 2(e), the XPS spectrum of Bi4f displays two characteristic peaks located at 164.3 and 159.2 eV, which are attributed to the binding energies of Bi4f5/2 and Bi4f7/2 orbitals in Bi3+ [35]. After introducing N-CQDs, the peak position shifted towards higher binding energy, indicating a chemical interaction between N-CQDs and UBWO. A similar phenomenon was observed in the XPS spectrum of W4f in Fig. 2(f), where the two peaks of W4f shifted towards higher binding energies after the introduction of N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2(g), the XPS spectrum of O1s exhibits two distinct peaks at 530.3 and 531.3 eV which can be attributed to lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen or C=O band. The peak at 532.7 eV in 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO may originate from the C-O band in N-CQDs [36]. The XPS spectrum of C1s in Fig. 2(h) shows four peaks at 284.8, 285.6, 286.7, and 288.0 eV, corresponding to the C–C/C=C, C-O, C-N, and C=O bonds in N-CQDs, respectively [37]. As shown in Fig. 2(i), the XPS spectra of N1s in 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO exhibit two peaks at 399.8 and 400.6 eV, respectively, attributed to the C-N and N–H bonds in N-CQDs [38]. The above results indicate the successful synthesis of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO with strong chemical interaction between N-CQDs and UBWO which is conducive to promoting the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers through the material’s interface.

TC was used as anantibiotic pollutant model to evaluate the photocatalytic efficiency of different catalysts under visible light. As presented in Fig. 3 (a), even after long-term irradiation, the concentration of TC solution remains almost unchanged without the addition of catalysts, indicating that the photodegradation of TC itself can be ignored. The PBWO can remove 53 % of TC in 40 min, while UBWO can remove 71 % of TC under visible light, indicating that ultrathin UBWO has higher efficiency in TC degradation than PBWO. Then, the degradation performance of TC by N-CQD/UBWO was evaluated. As given in Fig. 3(a), the degradation rate of TC by N-CQD/UBWO increases first and then decreases with the increase of CQDs loading, possibly due to excessive N-CQDs hindering the light absorption of Bi2WO6 and reducing the number of active reaction sites [39]. When the added amount of N-CQDs is 21 mg, the prepared 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO achieved the optimal degradation rate of TC (85 %) within 40 min.

Meanwhile, reaction kinetics for TC degradation was fitted through the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model by using the following equation (Eq. (1):
(1)
- l n
C t
C 0
= k t
where Ct denotes the TC concentration at time t while C0 representsinitial concentration of TC, and k is the degradation rate constant. As depicted in Figs. S5 and 3(b), the 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO for TC degradation exhibits the optimum first-order rate constant (k = 0.07116 min−1), which is 2.6 times greater than that of pure PBWO (k = 0.0272 min−1) and 20 times greater than that of N-CQDs (k = 0.00357 min−1). Fig. 3(c) presents the changes in UV–vis spectra of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO degradation solution under different irradiation times. With the progress of reaction time, the absorption intensity located at 356 nm which can be assigned to the maximum absorbance of TC gradually decreases, indicating that TC molecules are gradually degraded under visible light illumination. Furthermore, Fig. S6 shows that the mineralization efficiency of TC by 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO is 38.83 %, which is 1.7 times higher than that of pure UBWO (22.85 %) after 1 h of visible light irradiation. The results indicate that 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO is more effective in mineralizing TC molecules. In addition, the reusability of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO for TC degradation was evaluated through four recycling experiments. As displayed in Fig. 3(d), although a slight decrease in its photocatalytic efficiency was observed, 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO still maintained an 80 % of TC removal rate after four runs, which may be attributed to the uncleaned degradation intermediates covering the active sites on the catalyst surface [40]. FT-IR spectra Fig. S7 shows that the structure of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO does not undergo significant changes after cyclic uses, indicating that the prepared 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO has favorable stability and reusability.

To better explore the potential of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO for photocatalytic removal of antibiotics in complex water environments, the effects of coexisting anions and humic acid (HA) on the removal efficiency of TC were studied. As presented in Fig. 3(e), compared with ultrapure water, HA, SO4 2-, NO3 –, and Cl- have a slight effect on the degradation of TC, possibly because they act as scavengers of free radicals or holes, producing lower reactive free radicals [41]. The effect of theinitial pH value on the TC removal performance by 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO is shown in Fig. 3(f). Compared to weakly acidic environments (pH values between 2.9–5.0), weakly alkaline environments (pH values between 7.1–11.0) are more conducive to the degradation of TC. As known, TC has three pKa values (pK a1 = 3.3, pK a2 = 7.7 and pK a3 = 9.7), indicating that it exhibits four molecular species at different pH values, namely cationic TC+ species (pH < 3.3), zwitterionic TC species (3.3 < pH < 7.7), anionic TC− species (7.7 < pH < 9.7), and dianionic TC2− species (pH > 9.7) [42]. The point of zero charge of 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO is 6.2 (Fig. S8), indicating that the catalyst surface is positively charged at pH < 6.2, and there is electrostatic repulsion between it and cationic/zwitterionic TC, resulting in a decrease in TC degradation efficiency. However, the potential of OH–/·OH decreases when the pH value is greater than 7.1 [43], leading to the production of more ·OH radicals through direct oxidation of H2O over 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO photocatalytic system, thereby maintaining favorable TC degradation efficiency. Therefore, the degradation efficiency of TC is closely related to the surface properties of the catalyst and the pH value of the water environment.

Besides, the universality of 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO was evaluated by degrading CIP (Fig. S9), which shows that it can effectively remove 88.0 % of CIP within 1 h under visible light, demonstrating its great potential for eliminating other antibiotics in water environments. As depicted in Table S1, the photodegradation performance of 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO for TC is superior to most similar photocatalysts previously reported. This fact indicates that 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO has great potential for treating wastewater-containing antibiotics.

The designed 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO was evaluated for its effectiveness in removing TC under monochromatic light at 365, 420, 520, 600, 700, and 800 nm. As presented in Fig. 4 (a), the shorter the wavelength, resulted in the better removal efficiencies of TC. The greater energy of short-wavelength light excites 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO, resulting in the generation of more photo-generated carriers that contribute to the degradation of TC. The rate constants of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO for TC degradation under different illumination wavelengths were fitted based on a first-order kinetic model. As displayed in Fig. 4(b-c), 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO exhibited certain degradation of TC under different illumination wavelengths (365–700 nm), with the maximum rate constant at 365 nm of k = 0.03280 min−1, and the minimum rate constant at 700 nm of k = 0.00282 min−1. To explain the performance of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO towards TC photocatalytic degradation under abroad spectrum, the up-conversion spectra of N-CQDs were tested. As displayed in Fig. 4 (d), the N-CQDs absorb light at 600–900 nm and can emit light at 380–600 nm, demonstrating excellent up-conversion performance. Fig. 5 (a) shows that the absorption edge of UBWO is only at 450 nm, and it cannot respond to long visible wavelengths or even near-infrared light. Due to the excellent up-conversion performance of N-CQDs, when N-CQDs are combined with UBWO, N-CQDs absorb light at 600–900 nm and emit light with a wavelength less than 450 nm to excite UBWO. Therefore, 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO exhibits favorable photocatalytic removal performance of TC in a wide visible light range. To further demonstrate the photocatalytic performance of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO under a wide spectrum, the in-situ EPR spectra of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO were tested under 700 nm wavelength light irradiation. As presented in Fig. 4(e-f), 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO showed no free radicals signal in the dark. After being irradiated with 700 nm light for 10 min, 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO produced significant signals of ·OH and ·O2 – free radicals, confirming the broad spectral response characteristics of the 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO.

Different catalysts' optical properties and band structureswere measured to explain the reasons fortheir enhanced photocatalytic activities. As presented in Fig. 5(a), UV–vis DRS spectra show that 3 wt%N-CCQDs/UBWO exhibits enhanced light absorption in the range of 200–800 nm compared to the original PBWO and UBWO, indicating that the 3 wt%N-CCDDs/UBWO heterojunction can better absorb light. Besides, Fig. 5 (b) and S10 show that the optical bandgaps of UBWO and N-CQDs are 2.73 and 1.81 eV according to the Kubelka Munk formula. The XPS-VB spectra reveal that the VB positions of N-CQDs and UBWO are 3.19 and 2.31 eV, respectively (Fig. 5c). Using the formula: (
E VB-NHE
=Φ+
E VB-XPS
- 4.44 ), where Φ is the electronic work function of the instrument (4.20 eV) [44], the VB positions (vs. NHE) of N-CQDs and UBWO were estimated to be 2.95 and 2.07 V, respectively. Therefore, the CB positions (vs. NHE) of N-CQDs and UBWO are found to be 1.14 and −0.66 V according to the formula: Eg = EVB-ECB. Thus, the band structures of UBWO and N-CQDs were obtained as shown in Fig. S11, indicating a favorable match between UBWO and N-CQDs. This suggests that S-scheme heterojunction can be formed between UBWO and N-CQDs, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic removal of antibiotics by the resulting catalyst.

To further study the photo/electrochemical features of catalysts, the carrier separation efficiency and transfer ability of the designed UBWO and 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO were studied using techniques such as steady-state PL, electrochemical impedance and photocurrent responses. As shown in Fig. 5(d), 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO has lower PL intensity than the original UBWO and N-CQDs, indicating that the heterojunction formed by UBWO and N-CQDs has favorable photogenerated charges separation ability [45]. The photocurrent responses further elucidate the generation and transfer characteristics of photogenerated charges. As shown in Fig. 5(e), after the formation of a heterojunction between UBWO and N-CQDs, the photogenerated carrier recombination over the catalyst is suppressed, and more electrons/holes migrate to its surface under light illumination, resulting in higher transient photocurrent signals [46]. In addition, Fig. 5(f) shows the electrochemical impedance curve of 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO, which has a smaller arc radius compared to the original PBWO and UBWO, indicating lower charge transfer resistance [47]. The above results indicate that UBWO and N-CQDs may form heterojunctions, improving the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes, and allowing more photogenerated carriers transportation to the surface of heterojunction, thereby generating more active species and promoting the photocatalytic degradation of TC.

The difference in work function (Φ) is the standard for evaluating the direction of electron transfer between two semiconductor materials [48]. Thus, to comprehend the charge transfer mechanism and intrinsic driving force between UBWO and N-CQDs, their Φ were investigated utilizing theUPS technique. As presented in Fig. 6 (a), the secondary electron cutoff energies (Ecutoff) of N-CQDs and UBWO obtained from the tangent of the UPS spectrum are 15.83 and 16.62 eV, respectively. According to the formula: Φ = 21.22 eV–Ecutoff (21.22 eV is the energy of the excitation source HeI) [49], the calculated Φ of N-CQDs and UBWO (vs. vacuum energy level) are 5.39 and 4.56 eV, respectively, indicating that the Fermi level (EF) of N-CQDs is lower than that of UBWO. As displayed in Fig. 6(d), when N-CQDs and UBWO are in close contact, due to the difference in EF, the free electrons in UBWO spontaneously flow towards N-CQDs until the same EF is reached at the interface of thecatalyst. As a result, UBWO and N-CQDs exhibit charge depletion and accumulation regions, respectively. This electron transfer pathway generates an intrinsic electric field (IEF) pointing from UBWO to N-CQDs, while bending the energy band at the interface. Under visible light irradiation, electrons in CB of N-CQDs can recombine with holes in VB of UBWO under the action of interface IEF, band bending, and Coulomb force, thereby achieving efficient separation of photogenerated carriers in 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO heterojunction while maintaining strong photocatalytic oxidation–reduction ability. To provide sufficient evidence for the S-scheme photogenerated charge transfer pathway, in-situ XPS tests were conducted on 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO under illumination. As given in Fig. 6(b-c), the binding energy of XPS W4f orbitals in 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO shifts towards the lower direction under illumination. On the contrary, the binding energies of C-N/C-O and C=O bonds in XPS C1s orbitals shift toward higher directions, indicating electron transfer from N-CQDs to UBWO under illumination. The UPS and in-situ XPS analysis fully evidence that a 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO S-scheme heterojunction is formed between UBWO and N-CQDs, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic degradation of TC.

The potential active species generated during the photocatalytic process over 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO were determined using in-situ EPR technology. As revealed in Fig. 7 (a-b), the 3 wt% N-CQDs/UBWO did not detect signals of DMPO- ⋅OH and DMPO- ⋅O2 – signals in the dark. However, under visible light, a DMPO- ⋅OH quadruple peak with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 was distinctly observed, and six strong signal peaks of DMPO- ⋅O2 – were also detected, indicating that 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO can simultaneously produce ⋅OH and ⋅O2 – radicals under visible light. Within the prolongation of irradiation time, the intensity of DMPO-⋅OH and DMPO- ∙O2 – signals relatively increases, indicating that the amount of ⋅OH and ⋅O2 – radicals increases with the prolongation of irradiation time. The above findings indicate that UBWO and an appropriate amount of N-CQDs form a favorable heterojunction, which can generate more abundant active species including ⋅OH, and ⋅O2 – radicals under visible light, thereby promoting the photocatalytic removal of TC. To study the role of active species in the photodegradation of TC, quenching experiments of active species were conducted. As shown in Fig. 7(c), after adding ascorbic acid (AA) to the photocatalytic reaction, the degradation efficiency of TC was reduced to 34 %, indicating that ⋅O2 – is the main active species for TC photodegradation. Meanwhile, the addition of sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) and isopropanol (IPA) also has a certain inhibitory effect on the degradation of TC, suggesting that h+ and ⋅OH also play a positive role in the photodegradation of TC. As presented in Fig. 7 (d) and S12, when AA, Na2C2O4, and IPA were added, the first-order reaction rate constants for TC degradation were respectively found to be 0.00556, 0.02579, and 0.05563 min−1, further indicating that ⋅O2 – is the main active species for the photocatalytic removal of TC, followed by h+ and ⋅OH.

Based on the previous work function calculation, in-situ XPS analysis, in-situ EPR and photocatalytic experiments, it is speculated that the charge transfer mechanism of S-scheme in the 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO is not type II. As shown in Fig. 7(e), the e- transfers from the CB of UBWO to the CB of N-CQDs, while h+ in the VB of N-CQDs transfers to the VB of UBWO, which to some extent promotes the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. In this case, the CB position of N-CQDs is corrected to 1.14 V compared to O2/⋅O2 – (−0.33 V vs. NHE) [50]. Therefore, the e- in N-CQDs CB cannot reduce O2 to ⋅O2 – anion radicals. The in-situ EPR and quenching experiment show that the most active species in the 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO photocatalytic degradation of TC is ⋅O2 –, which contradicts the traditional II type photocatalytic mechanism and the formation of ⋅O2 –. Thus, based on the above analysis, anS-scheme charge transfer mechanism is proposed. As depicted in Fig. 7(f), the e- in CB of N-CQDs and h+ in VB of UBWO undergo recombination under visible light, resulting in strong reducibility of e- in CB of UBWO exhibits strong reduction and strong oxidization of h+ in VB of N-CQDs, leading to the generation of a large number of ⋅O2 – and ⋅OH reactive species and promoting the degradation of TC. It should be noted that due to the strong up-conversion properties of N-CQDs, they act as energy converters, absorbing long-wavelength light (600 nm < λ1 < 800 nm) and emitting short-wavelength light (380 nm < λ2 < 600 nm). Then, the converted short wavelength light (λ2) excites the e- transition from VB to CB of UBWO. Meanwhile, N-CQD itself can also be excited, causing theseparation of some electrons and holes in photocatalytic reactions. In summary, the possible reaction process for photocatalytic degradation of TC over the 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO S-scheme heterojunction is given as follows (Eqs. 2–7):

To elucidate the relevant pathways of the 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO S-scheme heterojunction for TC degradation, degradation solutions were collected at 0, 20, and 40 min of light exposure for LC-MS analysis to distinguish the intermediate products of TC. As presented in Fig. S13, the total MS spectra of possible intermediate products and degradation solutions after different light exposure times show that the peak of TC molecules (m/z = 455) gradually decreases with the prolongation of light exposure time, and many new peaks appear in MS spectra. With further light exposure, these peaks gradually decrease, indicating that TC is degraded into small molecule products, CO2, and H2O by the 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO. To better investigate the photodegradation pathways of TC, the TC charge distribution and Fukui index were used to predict the sites of reactive species attacks. Fig. 8 (a) shows the optimized molecular structure of TC using DFT. The optimized LUMO and HOMO orbitals corresponding to the TC structure are given in Fig. 8b which reveals the sites in TC molecules that are susceptible to nucleophilic and electrophilic attacks, respectively. The HOMO of TC is located at the C12-C66 ring and N-methyl group, while the LUMO is located at the C1-C2 ring and C14 ring. To accurately quantify the susceptibility of each atomic site to electrophilic, nucleophilic, and radical attacks, the Fukui index and charge distribution of TC molecule were calculated by way of DFT. As depicted in Fig. 8(c), positions C9, O20, O25, O27, N30, and C31 display high f - values, indicating greater susceptibility to h+ attacks [51]; C2, C10, C16, O25, O27, and O29 positions show high f + values, which are susceptible to preferential attacks by nucleophilic free radicals ·O2 – [52]; C2, C16, O20, O25, O27, and 30 N positions show higher f 0 values, indicating that these points are susceptible to preferential radial attacks by ·OH [53].

Based on LC-MS and DFT calculations, two different degradation pathways for TC molecule were presented, as shown in Fig. 9 . The main pathways of TC degradation include hydroxylation reaction, deamidation, demethylation, ring-opening reaction, dehydroxylation, and deamination. In pathway I; the C9=C14 double bond of TC (m/z = 445) is attacked by ·OH, causing a hydroxylation reaction and conversion to P1 (m/z = 461); Subsequently, P1 undergoes ·OH attack to remove N-methyl and hydroxyl groups, forming P2 (m/z = 399); Under the attack of h+ and ·O2 –, P2 undergoes deamidation, ring opening, dehydroxylation, and deamination to form P3 (m/z = 298) and P4 (m/z = 200) [54]. In pathway II; TC (m/z = 445) undergoes dehydroxylation under ·OH attack to produce P5 (m/z = 429); P5 undergoes demethylation to generate P6 (m/z = 415), which is attacked by ·O2 – and h+ to undergo deamidation, ring opening, and demethylation to form P7 (m/z = 374) and P8 (m/z = 316); Under the gradual attack of ·OH and ·O2 – radicals, P8 decomposes into P9 (m/z = 302) and P10 (m/z = 279) [55]. Finally, these intermediates with a molecular weight greater than 200 are converted into smaller molecules including P11 (m/z = 149), P12 (m/z = 130), P13 (m/z = 114), P14 (m/z = 100), P15 (m/z = 55), P16 (m/z = 74), and P17 (m/z = 60), some of them are mineralized into CO2 and H2O molecules through ·O2 –, ·OH, and h+ attacks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity assessment of TC degradation intermediates
ce:section.ce:para: Using QSAR analysis and T.E.S.T. software were employed to predict the biological toxicity of TC and its degraded products [56]. Fig. 10 (a) displays that the LC50 values of most intermediates are higher than TC after degradation, among which the LC50 values of the final products P17, P14, P15, P10, P16, and P12 > 100 mg/L, indicating a decrease in acute toxicity after photocatalytic degradation of TC. Fig. 10(b) further reveals the developmental toxicity of TC and its intermediates. Exceptfor P2 and P3 TC by-products, the developmental toxicity of all other generated intermediates is reduced compared to TC, indicating a decrease in developmental toxicity after its degradation. Fig. 10(c) shows that except forseven intermediates with positive mutagenicity, the other ten intermediates have no mutagenicity. From the above biological toxicity prediction, it can be seen that the toxicity of TC significantly decreases after its photocatalytic degradation by 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO S-scheme heterojunction.

Furthermore, the toxicity changes before and after TC degradation were evaluated by culturing mung bean sprouts in distinct TC solution systems. As shown in Fig. 10(d), mung beans cultured in tap water effectively germinated and grew well. Meanwhile, although mung beans cultured in TC solution effectively germinated, their growth condition was poor, and the roots of the bean sprouts turned yellow and withered (Fig. 10e). On the contrary, mung beans cultured in TC solution treated with 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO not only effectively germinated, but also grew well (Fig. 10f), which was relatively lower than the mung bean control group treated with tap water as the culture medium. This may be due to the residual small amount of TC in the solution after its photocatalytic degradation. The above findings indicate that the TC solution treated with 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO for 40 min has low biological toxicity, further verifying its feasibility in treating antibiotics in actual water environments under visible light irradiation.

In summary, an innovative 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO ultrathin S-scheme heterojunction was successfully constructed by incorporating N-CQDs derived from bio-waste lignin into the UBWO system utilizing the hydrothermal method. The ultrathin structure shortens the distance of photogenerated charge transfer and reduces their probability of recombination. The significant up-conversion property of N-CQD endows 3 wt%N-CQDs/UBWO a broad spectral response characteristic, as evidenced by the ∙O2 – and ∙OH radicals generation under 700 nm light illumination. Besides, in-situ XPS, EPR and work function analysis proved the S-scheme charge transfer route between N-CQDs and UBWO materials, endowing the composite system with strong photocatalytic redox and charge space separation abilities. The resultant strong ∙O2 –, ∙OH and h+ active species were generated in 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO under visible light, promoting the degradation of TC, with ∙O2 – radicals playing an important role in its degradation. Therefore, the optimized 3 wt%N-CQD/UBWO S-scheme heterojunction degraded 85.0 % of TC within 40 min under visible light, with first-order rate constants 2.6 and 20.3 times greater than that of UBWO and N-CQDs, respectively. Referring to the Fukui function calculations and HPLC-MS analysis, three possible degradation pathways for TC were proposed. The T.E.S.T. software prediction and mung bean cultivation results demonstrate that the intermediate products of TC degradation are low-toxic or even non-toxic. This work has opened up ideas for designing high cost-effectiveness S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts by employing lignin based-CQDs to boost efficient and green treatment of antibiotics in water.
